_header.purpose: Observability stack summary dashboard displaying essential signals for infrastructure health assessment
_header.scope: High-level EC2 and container resource metrics with links to detail dashboards in the Observability folder
_header.overview: Provides a top-level summary of observability infrastructure health using stat panels for EC2 CPU, Memory, Load Average, and Disk Usage, plus a time series showing container CPU usage by service name. Includes a Data Availability status panel. Serves as the entry point for the Observability folder, linking to Saturation, USE, and Profiling dashboards for drill-down investigation.
_header.dependencies: Mimir datasource (uid: mimir), node_exporter metrics (node_cpu_seconds_total, node_memory_MemAvailable_bytes, node_memory_MemTotal_bytes, node_load1, node_filesystem_avail_bytes, node_filesystem_size_bytes), cAdvisor metrics (container_cpu_usage_seconds_total)
_header.interfaces: PromQL queries against node_exporter and cAdvisor metric names stored in Mimir
_header.related: home.json for top-level navigation, obs-saturation.json, obs-use.json, obs-profiling.json
_header.implementation: Grafana dashboard JSON schema version 39 with stat and time series panels for infrastructure metrics

title: Summary
description: Is the observability infrastructure itself healthy? This dashboard monitors the EC2 instance running the observability stack (Grafana, Mimir, Loki, Tempo, Pyroscope, Alloy). If this machine is resource-starved, your monitoring data will have gaps or delays. The stat panels show instant resource usage — all green means the stack has headroom. Any panel turning yellow or red means the observability stack is under pressure: metrics may be delayed, queries may time out, and dashboards may show stale data. Check the container CPU breakdown to identify which service is consuming the most resources.

options.content:
# Observability Stack Summary
Is the observability infrastructure itself healthy? This monitors the EC2 instance running Grafana, Mimir, Loki, Tempo, Pyroscope, and Alloy. If this machine is resource-starved, your monitoring data will have gaps or delays. All green means the stack has headroom. Check the container CPU breakdown to identify which service is consuming the most resources.

panels.title: EC2 CPU %
panels.description: Current CPU utilization of the observability EC2 instance. Green (<70%) means plenty of headroom. Yellow (70-90%) means the stack is working hard — consider whether recent traffic increases are temporary. Red (>90%) means queries will slow down and metric ingestion may lag.

panels.title: EC2 Memory %
panels.description: Current memory utilization of the observability EC2 instance. The observability stack (especially Mimir and Tempo) is memory-intensive. A steady climb over days suggests a memory leak in one of the services. Red (>90%) means OOM kill risk — the Linux kernel may terminate a container, causing data loss.

panels.title: Load Average (1m)
panels.description: 1-minute load average — the number of processes waiting for CPU time. Compare against the number of CPU cores: a load of 2 on a 2-core machine means fully utilized. Load exceeding core count (yellow/red) means processes are queuing and performance degrades. High load with low CPU% suggests I/O wait (disk bottleneck).

panels.title: Disk Usage %
panels.description: Root filesystem usage on the observability EC2 instance. Mimir, Loki, and Tempo all store data on disk. A steadily climbing value means data retention is filling the disk. Red (>90%) is critical — a full disk will crash the observability stack. Consider adjusting retention periods or increasing the EBS volume size.

panels.title: Container CPU Usage by Service
panels.description: CPU consumption broken down by Docker container (Mimir, Loki, Tempo, Grafana, Alloy, etc.). Identifies which observability service is consuming the most CPU. If one service dominates, it may need resource limits adjusted or its workload reduced. During an incident where dashboards are slow, this panel shows whether a specific service is overloaded.

options.content:
### Infrastructure Metrics Status
This dashboard uses **node_exporter** (`node_*`) and **cAdvisor** (`container_*`) metrics scraped by Alloy. Metrics refresh every 30 seconds.
